title: Replicated checklist static groups use per-device random group keys
description: replicated_checklist currently generates a fresh random GroupKey when inserting the configured static group. Devices bootstrapping the same configured group independently therefore persist different symmetric group secrets under the same group id, so reliable delivery works but group broadcast AEAD open fails. Add a temporary explicit group-secret-password config field, derive the group key from that password plus group identity context, and reject existing local stores whose persisted group key does not match the configured derived key.

title: Validate replicated checklist without static routes
description: Update replicated-checklist configuration, docs, and acceptance coverage so the application can be run without any static-peer-routes entries. Static routes should remain accepted as optional hints, but the documented custom UDP discovery flow should show two peers finding usable direct UDP routes and completing the existing checklist sync scenarios without preconfigured peer endpoints.

title: Wire custom discovery into replication runtime
description: Replace the runtime's custom-discovery gap with a composed route source that can consume custom UDP peer tracker route candidates and still accept static-peer-routes as optional hints. The replicated runtime should no longer require flotsync.replication.runtime.static-peer-routes for basic live direct-UDP operation when peers are discoverable on the local network.

title: Implement custom UDP peer tracker route source
description: Implement the non-mDNS peer discovery receive/tracking side for the custom UDP path. It should listen for custom peer announcements, validate the announcement shape defined by the PeerDiscovery+Tracking protocol task, maintain Known/Reachable/Stale state for configured group members, and publish UDP route candidates through the replication RouteDiscoveryPort shape instead of only broadcasting outbound announcements.

title: Complete custom UDP discovery for replicated checklist
description: Tracking slice for making custom, non-mDNS peer discovery usable by the replication runtime. Completion criterion: the replicated_checklist application can run without any flotsync.replication.runtime.static-peer-routes entries; static-peer-routes become optional hints rather than required for functionality. Scope is the fully custom UDP path first. mDNS browser/public-message tasks are explicitly out of scope for this slice.
notes: Agreed custom-only sequence: S01 fix flotsync-54m7 CLI shutdown handle ownership; S02 complete flotsync-31m PeerDiscovery+Tracking protocol/auth rules for the custom path; S03 complete flotsync-brt Kompact interface/path for custom announcements; S04 complete flotsync-0mi socket reuse configuration where needed for custom UDP discovery; S05 complete flotsync-gxle custom UDP peer tracker route source; S06 complete flotsync-ol4e runtime wiring so custom-discovered routes and static hints are composed; S07 complete flotsync-5hvw replicated-checklist validation without static-peer-routes. mDNS tasks flotsync-dnt and flotsync-2ss are intentionally out of scope for this slice.

title: Enable public replication runtime load with store-provisioned security
description: Remove the temporary public-loader SecurityUnavailable fail-fast path. Normal replication load should construct DeliverySecurity from already-provisioned ReplicationStore security records plus the caller/device-local store-secret key material, then build the runtime host/topology through the same path currently used by tests. This task is intentionally independent of replicated-checklist setup: replication should not care whether security records were provisioned by checklist setup, a test harness, or another application. It is also separate from replacing the stored local private-key/JWKS representation; this task only makes the production runtime load consume the provisioned security state instead of requiring test-only injection.
design:
SEC11-01. Add an explicit public security load input, separate from ReplicationConfig, so secret-bearing state is not placed into a clone/debug/equality policy struct. Use a small load-time security input carrying the store-secret key id and device-local StoreSecretKey handle with redacted Debug.

SEC11-02. Change the public runtime loaders to require that input and build DeliverySecurity in the normal path: load local member identity from the store, load DeliverySecurity from store-provisioned records and the supplied store-secret key, validate persisted group security readiness, then start DeliveryRuntimeHost through the same runtime construction path used by tests.

SEC11-03. Remove the temporary SecurityUnavailable fail-fast loader. Replace LoadError::SecurityUnavailable with a real security-loading error variant wrapping DeliverySecurityError with application context.

SEC11-04. Move DeliverySecurity::load, local private-key opening, local-private-key error variants, and EncryptedStoreSecret::to_store_secret_ciphertext out from test-support cfg. Deterministic provisioning helpers remain test-only.

SEC11-05. Reuse test support only to provision store records, then exercise the public loader. The core success test proves provisioned local private keys plus the correct store-secret key starts the runtime without test-only DeliverySecurity injection.

SEC11-06. During public runtime load, validate security for every persisted replication group in the store. After loading DeliverySecurity, load all ReplicationGroupRecords, build GroupMembers from each record's canonical members, and require public keys for every member needed by those groups. The local member can be satisfied by the loaded local private keys; every non-local group member must have a trusted public-key record that decodes successfully. If any group cannot be made usable, fail load with clear LoadError context naming the group/member/security problem. Empty stores still load with only local private keys; create/bootstrap paths continue to validate trusted keys for newly introduced members.

SEC11-07. Remove dead-code allowances that only existed because the public loader returned immediately.
acceptance_criteria: Public load no longer returns LoadError::SecurityUnavailable when required security inputs are supplied; DeliverySecurity loading/opening is available outside test-support; an explicit load-time security input carries the device-local store-secret key material or handle without parsing application config inside flotsync_security; normal runtime host construction receives a mandatory DeliverySecurity; missing local private keys, invalid/wrong store-secret material, or missing trusted public keys for persisted replication-group members fail with clear LoadError context; empty stores can load with only local private keys; broad dead_code allowances caused by the fail-fast loader are removed where the production path makes code reachable again; tests cover successful public runtime load from provisioned store records and failure cases for missing security state.

title: Persist reliable outbound state and bound sealed bootstrap fan-out
description: Follow-up from flotsync-sec.4. Reliable bootstrap payloads can eventually contain substantial group state, so reliable delivery must not build and retain every per-recipient sealed bootstrap message in memory. Combine the prerequisite and the fan-out fix in one slice: persist reliable outbound pending-send state first, then change bootstrap fan-out so it stores recipient work durably and seals at most one or a bounded set of envelopes at a time. Retry and restart handling should resume from persisted sender work instead of requiring all sealed envelopes to remain in memory.
acceptance_criteria: Reliable delivery persists pending outbound sender work needed to resume retries after runtime restart; bootstrap fan-out stores recipient work durably before transport submission; sealing is performed lazily or in a bounded batch instead of pre-sealing every recipient envelope; retry and recipient-ack timeout paths can reconstruct or continue work from persisted state; tests cover restart/resume, bounded sealing behaviour, retry after transient send failure, and completion cleanup.
notes: Explore the idea of a Multicast-like request type to reliable delivery so that the individual messages don't have to be created all at once at the replication component level.

title: Improve test socket reservation to avoid stuck replication tests
description: Runtime and integration tests still sometimes need manual intervention to kill stuck runs when reserved sockets or runtime-host endpoint bindings do not unwind cleanly. Improve the socket reservation/broker path so tests fail fast with useful diagnostics instead of hanging: track live reservations and activated bindings, make cleanup idempotent, detect leaked or unreleased bindings at test shutdown, and ensure runtime-host tests cannot wait forever on a reserved UDP endpoint that was not rebound or released correctly.
design:
JZO-01. Stay process-local for this task.

JZO-02. Add a progress_epoch to the existing ReservedSocketBrokerState.

JZO-03. Count only meaningful local progress: fulfilled request, lease release, OS socket acquired, idle socket consumed, partial acquisition returned, idle trim after demand changes.

JZO-04. Do not count wakeups, sleeps, lock acquisition, or repeated bind failures.

JZO-05. Replace the normal acquire failure trigger with a no-progress stall window. If progress keeps happening, keep waiting. If no meaningful local progress happens and this request still cannot acquire its declared slots, fail early.

JZO-06. Keep a large absolute timeout only as a safety fuse.

JZO-07. Diagnostics should include request id, requested/acquired counts, next needed kind, waiting/idle counts, active acquisition owner, last progress event, last bind error, and slot summary.

JZO-08. If there are no local holders and OS acquisition keeps failing, the error should say local acquisition is stuck and full cross-binary holder diagnosis is deferred to flotsync-7hp0.
acceptance_criteria: The process-local reserved-socket broker tracks a progress epoch for meaningful acquisition/release progress; waiting reservations reset their stall timer when progress advances; repeated OS bind failures and condvar wakeups do not count as progress; a reservation fails early when no local progress happens for the stall window; a larger absolute timeout remains only as a safety fuse; failure diagnostics include request/acquisition counts, next needed kind, waiting/idle counts, active owner, last progress event, last bind error, and slot summary; tests cover progress reset, no-progress stall, bind failures not counting as progress, and release/idle return counting as progress.
close_reason: Completed local socket reservation progress tracking and diagnostics

title: Provision replicated-checklist security state during setup
description: For this MVP slice, the replicated-checklist example owns application-side security provisioning before replication starts. The example reads its temporary plaintext local database secret plus local private JWKS and trusted public JWKS paths from application config, parses key material through flotsync_security, validates the configured static group members, and writes the resulting group-security state into ReplicationStore during ensure_configured_group. Replication runtime then reads security material from the store like normal group metadata.
design:
S710-04/10 agreed implementation plan for temporary replicated-checklist store provisioning.

1. Temporary replication API: add a public provisioning helper in flotsync_replication that owns the store-record and encryption details while the example owns config/files/setup policy. Mark the helper as temporary MVP setup pending flotsync-uohh; long-term we do not want an application-side provisioning step.

2. Local and trusted key provisioning: the helper accepts the local member id, ReplicationSecuritySecrets, local private JWKS text, and trusted public JWKS texts. It parses through flotsync_security, seals local private keys into LocalMemberPrivateKeysRecord, stores trusted public keys as TrustedMemberPublicKeysRecord values, and returns parsed identities so the example can validate static group coverage.

3. Initial group material: add a temporary public helper that prepares EncryptedGroupSecurityMaterial for the initial static group from a GroupKey and ReplicationSecuritySecrets. This keeps encrypted-cell contexts out of the example.

4. replicated-checklist setup flow: ensure_configured_group reads key files from config, provisions local/trusted security state before inserting the static group, validates that every configured group member has trusted public keys except the local member, creates the initial group security material, and then inserts the group record.

5. Idempotency and diagnostics: repeated setup with identical local/trusted material should be accepted; missing, malformed, duplicate, or mismatched key files should fail setup with clear example-level errors.

6. Validation: add focused tests proving setup writes security records that runtime load accepts, and that malformed or incomplete key files fail before runtime start. flotsync_security must remain free of application TOML/Kompact parsing.
acceptance_criteria: replicated-checklist config includes the temporary plaintext local database secret and local/trusted JWKS paths; ensure_configured_group validates and provisions security state into ReplicationStore before runtime start; malformed or mismatched local/trusted key files fail setup with clear errors; runtime startup loads provisioned group security state from ReplicationStore; and flotsync_security still does not parse application TOML or Kompact config.
close_reason: Completed temporary replicated-checklist security provisioning setup; reviewed and validated.

title: Add end-to-end validation for secure replication envelopes
description: Add end-to-end and focused integration validation for the flotsync_security MVP after the crate, config, store, bootstrap, group traffic, reliable ack, and example slices exist. The tests should prove that secure create-group bootstrap, secure group update delivery, secure summary/catch-up paths where applicable, and recipient ack verification work together through the runtime.
design:
S8R agreed implementation plan, restructure first.

S8R-01 Add a narrow public test-support surface behind the test-support feature rather than exposing broad runtime internals. The wrapper should let integration tests start runtimes with schemas and deterministic security, access the ReplicationApi, publish direct peer routes, inspect/wait for group membership, capture listener data-change events, and temporarily install groups for catch-up setup. Mark the temporary install helper as pending flotsync-sec.9.

S8R-02 Move security-oriented runtime end-to-end tests into flotsync_replication/tests/secure_runtime.rs: create-group bootstrap membership, secure publish delivery, update-gap NeedRange/UpdateBatch catch-up, and observed-summary catch-up.

S8R-03 Move public runtime security-loading tests into flotsync_replication/tests/security_loading.rs if the extraction stays clean. If this would require exposing inappropriate internals, leave that split for a follow-up and keep flotsync-sec.8 focused on secure runtime flows.

S8R-04 Keep delivery boundary tamper and recipient-ack spoofing tests local to delivery/group_broadcast and delivery/reliable_delivery. They use focused wire injection and component state and should not drive public test-support API shape.

S8R-05 After the restructure passes, add the missing end-to-end trust mismatch test in secure_runtime.rs: Bob has a trusted Alice record under Alice's identity but with wrong public key material; Alice creates a group; Bob must not install it. Use eventually/never helpers, not sleeps.

S8R-06 While moving tests, enforce socket-broker compliance: integration tests that start UDP runtime hosts reserve sockets up front and pass explicit runtime config.

S8R-07 Validate with cargo fmt --all, focused moved integration tests with -- --test-threads=1, focused delivery tests if test-support changes affect them, cargo check -p flotsync_replication --features test-support, cargo clippy -p flotsync_replication --features test-support -- -W clippy::pedantic, and git diff --check.
acceptance_criteria: Integration tests cover secure create-group bootstrap between peers, secure group update delivery, signature rejection, ciphertext rejection, public header authenticated-data rejection, trust mismatch rejection, and spoofed recipient ack rejection. Existing tests use deterministic flotsync_security test-support keys rather than placeholder plaintext signatures.

title: Add replicated-checklist key generation and secure config docs
description: Update replicated-checklist so users can generate local identity JWKS files through the CLI and configure secure peers without external crypto tooling. Extend example config parsing and docs for this MVP slice to include the temporary plaintext local database secret, local private identity JWKS path, and trusted public JWKS paths. Scenario docs should show first-run key generation and setup that provisions the store before replication starts.
acceptance_criteria: CLI exposes a key-generation command using flotsync_security; generated private/public JWKS files round-trip through example setup; example configs document temporary plaintext local database secret plus local/trusted JWKS paths; scenario docs show secure multi-peer setup and make clear the plaintext database secret is MVP-only pending keyring; tests cover relative/absolute key paths and config validation.
close_reason: Completed replicated-checklist key generation and secure config/docs setup; reviewed and validated.

title: Sign and verify reliable-delivery acknowledgements
description: Replace placeholder reliable-delivery recipient acknowledgement signatures with real Ed25519 signatures and verification. RecipientAck messages should sign the canonical ack public header and relevant delivery context. Sender-side reliable delivery must verify the ack against the expected recipient public key and message id before completing the sender work item.
design:
Agreed plan for flotsync-sec.6:

S6-01R Replace recipient ack `footer` with a reusable sealed-payload field. Generalise the previously added group sealed payload shape so group broadcast and recipient acks use the same content-plus-signature structure instead of keeping another detached footer path.

S6-02R For recipient acks, the sealed payload content may be empty unless implementation naturally needs an explicit signed body. The signature transcript must bind the distinct ack frame kind plus canonical `RecipientAckHeader` bytes, so the semantic ack context is authenticated.

S6-03R Add canonical recipient-ack public-header bytes in reliable-delivery wire code, matching the group-broadcast pattern.

S6-04R Add `DeliverySecurity` helpers for signing and verifying recipient acks using a distinct frame kind from reliable payloads and group broadcasts.

S6-05R Receiver side signs once after `processed` completes, caches the signed ack, and re-dispatches that signed ack on retries.

S6-06R Sender side verifies the ack matches the pending work item and verifies the recipient signature before marking the ack observed.

S6-07R Tests cover successful signed ack completion plus wrong message id, wrong original sender, wrong recipient, malformed sealed payload, and tampered signature/header rejection.

S6-08R Validation with focused reliable-delivery tests, formatting, and the relevant check/clippy pass.
acceptance_criteria: Recipient acknowledgements are signed by the recipient identity; sender-side reliable delivery rejects missing, malformed, wrong-recipient, wrong-message, and tampered ack signatures; existing reliable delivery completion only occurs after verification; and tests cover successful ack verification plus spoofed ack rejection.

title: Seal and verify group broadcast runtime payloads
description: Replace group-broadcast placeholder encrypted payload handling with flotsync_security group sealing and opening. Runtime outbound and inbound paths should load group security material from ReplicationStore, with sensitive columns decrypted through the store/database-secret layer. Outbound runtime messages should be encoded, sealed with the stored group symmetric key using ChaCha20-Poly1305 and derived nonce, signed with the local Ed25519 key, and sent through the existing delivery frame. Inbound group messages should keep shallow public-header routing, then verify the sender signature, open the AEAD payload with public header authenticated data, decode RuntimeMessage, and process only after all checks pass.
design:
Implement secure group-broadcast runtime payloads through the delivery security boundary.

Plan:

G01 Change group broadcast wire shape to match reliable delivery: the public header remains shallow-routeable, while the sealed payload contains ciphertext plus sender signature together. Avoid the existing split between encrypted_payload and footer for group envelopes.

G02 Add a typed group sealed payload in flotsync_security or flotsync_replication as appropriate, with ciphertext and sender signature as one value. flotsync_security should own transcript construction, signature creation/verification, AEAD seal/open, and stored group-key plaintext parsing.

G03 Keep group message overhead to the current MVP target: ChaCha20-Poly1305 tag in ciphertext plus one Ed25519 signature. Do not add per-message nonce, key id, epoch, or algorithm fields.

G04 Add stored group-secret open/parse support. DeliverySecurity should load the group record, open EncryptedStoreSecret with the configured store-secret key/context, parse the plaintext into a supported group cipher suite plus GroupKey, and reject unsupported suite, wrong key id/version/nonce length, malformed plaintext, missing group, or store access failures with Snafu errors.

G05 Make GroupMessageEnvelope explicit over payload state: GroupMessageEnvelope<PlaintextPayload> for runtime and catch-up submissions/deliveries, and GroupMessageEnvelope<SealedGroupPayload> for network wire. Do not keep placeholder footers in the group path.

G06 Put sealing/opening at the GroupBroadcastComponent boundary, because it owns the delivery message id and serves both runtime and catch-up traffic. Inject mandatory DeliverySecurity into the component through the host topology.

G07 Outbound submit flow: accept plaintext from runtime or catch-up, generate/keep the message id, load the stored group key for the target group, seal and sign with group context, convert to wire, and submit to the route transport. Self-delivery should also pass through successful sealing first, then deliver plaintext locally.

G08 Inbound flow: decode wire into GroupMessageEnvelope<SealedGroupPayload>, use the public header for shallow routing, load stored group security, verify sender signature, open AEAD payload with public header authenticated data, and emit GroupBroadcastDeliver with plaintext bytes only after checks pass.

G09 Runtime and catch-up code should continue to encode/decode RuntimeMessage bytes, but should read/write PlaintextPayload.bytes rather than ciphertext-shaped placeholders.

G10 Preserve the existing best-effort group broadcast semantics for this slice: on sealing/opening failures, log and drop rather than inventing new retry policy.

G11 Update protobuf/adapters/tests for the new sealed group payload shape. Cover successful secure group update delivery plus tampering failures for signature, public header/group context, and ciphertext. Keep summary, NeedRange, and UpdateBatch paths working after successful open.

G12 Validate with formatting, cargo checks, focused tests, clippy pedantic where practical, and a final check against this bd plan before declaring the task done.
acceptance_criteria: Group outbound paths no longer emit plaintext runtime payload bytes in secure mode; runtime uses group security material loaded from ReplicationStore rather than live application config; inbound group delivery rejects invalid signatures, tampered public headers, wrong group context, and tampered ciphertext; existing update, summary, NeedRange, and UpdateBatch paths operate after successful open; and tests cover secure group update delivery and tampering failures.
close_reason: Completed group broadcast payload security slice

title: Secure reliable bootstrap with HPKE and member public keys
description: Secure reliable bootstrap through a reusable delivery security boundary. flotsync_security owns crypto primitives for HPKE bootstrap payloads, signature transcripts, validation material, and encrypted store-secret sealing/opening. flotsync_replication adds a delivery-level security service that owns store access for local private keys, trusted public keys, database-secret encryption keys, bootstrap payload preparation, and inbound bootstrap opening. The runtime component should only provide semantic group/bootstrap intent and install or load ReplicationStore records; it must not construct HPKE transcripts or call flotsync_security bootstrap helpers directly. Reliable delivery should use the security service to seal per-recipient bootstrap envelopes and to open inbound bootstrap envelopes. Bootstrap payload includes group id, ordered members, every member signing public key, every member encryption public key, group cipher suite, and group symmetric key. Inbound bootstrap verifies sender signature, decrypts the HPKE payload, validates public keys against provisioned trusted public keys, writes encrypted group material, and installs group membership only after checks pass.
acceptance_criteria: Create-group outbound bootstrap seals per recipient through the delivery security service; inbound bootstrap rejects signature failures, HPKE decrypt failures, and trusted-key mismatches through that same service; stored group records contain encrypted sensitive group-security material and member public keys; runtime observes bootstrap state by loading group records from ReplicationStore and does not own crypto transcript construction; flotsync_security remains the only crate constructing bootstrap cryptographic transcripts; tests cover successful secure bootstrap plus mismatched public key and tampered payload failures.
close_reason: Completed secure reliable bootstrap delivery slice; reviewed, audited, and validated.

title: Store encrypted group secrets with replication group metadata
description: Extend ReplicationStore and the SQLite implementation so replication group records carry already-encrypted group-security material next to existing group metadata. The store must not own the device-local database secret or perform encryption itself: setup/runtime code passes opaque encrypted blobs into the store and later reads the same blobs back. Use an encrypted-cell shape with crypto version, key id, 24-byte XChaCha20-Poly1305 random nonce, and ciphertext. The group-secret authenticated data is canonically built from logical identifiers, not physical SQL names: logical table replication_group, logical column group_secret, logical row id group_id, key id, and crypto version. Add store-owned persistence for encrypted local member private keys and unencrypted trusted member public keys, because local private material is sensitive and trusted public credentials can be shared across groups without duplication. There are no legacy rows or migrations for this task; new storage is security-by-default.
acceptance_criteria: ReplicationGroupRecord carries required EncryptedGroupSecurityMaterial; SQLite schema initialisation creates non-null group-secret crypto_version, key_id, nonce, and ciphertext columns; insert/load/load_all/load_for_ids round-trip encrypted group material; the store API has records and transaction methods for encrypted local member private keys and trusted member public keys; trusted public key bytes are opaque Box<[u8]> values to keep cryptographic primitive sizes out of the store boundary; tests cover group material round-trips, local encrypted private-key round-trips, trusted public-key round-trips, and fixed helper material without plaintext or None placeholders; no migration, legacy-row, nullable-security-state, or store-owned-secret handling is introduced.

title: Abandoned: live replication security configuration
description: Abandoned in favour of store-provisioned security state. The previous live runtime configuration approach is no longer part of the MVP direction: storage layout work now comes first, and replicated-checklist setup will provision security material into ReplicationStore before replication starts.
acceptance_criteria: Closed as abandoned. Replacement work is tracked by flotsync-sec.10 after the store layout task.
close_reason: Abandoned: live typed replication security configuration is superseded by store-provisioned security state and a later replicated-checklist setup task.

title: Scaffold flotsync_security crate and core crypto API
description: Create the flotsync_security workspace crate with the crypto-only API surface for the MVP. The crate should own typed key material, jose-jwk based JWKS/JWK parsing, key role validation, identity key generation helpers, deterministic test-support identities, Ed25519ph sign/verify over streamed SHA-512 transcripts, ChaCha20-Poly1305 group seal/open, HPKE single-shot seal/open for X25519 bootstrap, nonce derivation, transcript construction, and Snafu-derived errors. It must not depend on Kompact, flotsync_io, delivery components, replication store internals, or application config parsing.
acceptance_criteria: The new flotsync_security crate builds independently; exposes typed APIs for identity generation, JWKS parsing, Ed25519ph sign/verify, group seal/open, and HPKE single-shot seal/open; includes deterministic test-support helpers; and has unit tests for key role rejection, round-trip key generation, sign/verify failure, group context tampering, ciphertext tampering, wrong context, and HPKE decrypt failure.
close_reason: Completed flotsync_security crate scaffold and core crypto API; reviewed and validated.

title: Add read tokens and replay-backed publish rebasing
description: Implement the manual replication slice fix for publishing local row mutations against the correct application read cut. Add an opaque public ReadToken carried by snapshot/listener APIs, change publish_changes to accept a request with that token plus row mutations, validate token/group scope in the runtime, persist enough row metadata to detect rows changed since the token, reconstruct row state at the token by replay when needed, generate CRDT operations from that base state, apply to latest local state, and persist/broadcast the update with read_versions from the token. Acceptance must cover the checklist failure mode where two peers concurrently edit different fields of the same row and sequential sync preserves both fields.
notes: Implemented read-token publish rebasing review fixes: receipt tokens now advance only the writer position from the request token; listener events carry per-update tokens and applications merge them; snapshot tokens are built from hosted group memberships; replay loading/materialisation moved into runtime/replay.rs and replay is scoped to touched rows; SQLite now requires row_last_changed_versions without schema migration fallback; checklist working set owns its read token. Validation: cargo test -p flotsync_replication -- --test-threads=1; cargo test -p flotsync_io_examples -- --test-threads=1; cargo test -p flotsync_core -- --test-threads=1; cargo test -p flotsync_data_types -- --test-threads=1.
close_reason: Implemented read-token publish rebasing and addressed review feedback

title: Document public ReplicationApi methods
description: High-priority follow-up in the manual replication slice. The public ReplicationApi trait methods are the main application-facing runtime boundary and need explicit docs covering what each method does, what state it reads or mutates, ordering/consistency expectations, error behaviour, and lifecycle constraints for returned providers.

title: T13 Exercise manual checklist concurrency scenarios
description: Use the replicated checklist example to exercise the manual slice scenarios: concurrent adds from two machines; one peer marks an item done while another renames it; one peer deletes an item while another edits it; one peer restarts and retains durable local state; and manual route configuration remains visible and testable without pretending discovery exists.

title: T12 Define the checklist CLI command surface
description:
Define and implement the minimal line-oriented replicated checklist CLI command surface for the manual replication slice. The CLI should be easy to run over SSH and script where practical, while keeping synchronisation explicitly manual.

Startup:

- Start from config only, e.g. replicated-checklist --config alice.toml.
- Do not pass --member separately. The config is node-specific and provides the local member identity.
- Config supplies local member identity, store path, local endpoint bind address, static group id, static ordered members, and static peer routes.
- On startup, install/load exactly one configured static group. If the durable store already has the group, it should match the configured members. If not, create the static local group record.

Group/member commands:

- No group creation, join, leave, or membership modification commands in this slice.
- members: print group id and ordered members.
- me: print local member identity, local endpoint/config summary, store path, and configured routes.

Item addressing:

- list displays both a current list index and the full UUID row id.
- Commands accept either the current list index or the full UUID.
- Indices resolve against the current materialised list at command time. This is good enough for the manual demo even though list order can change after mutations or sync.

Item commands:

- add <text>
- rename <item> <text>
- edit note <item>
- tag add <item> <tag>
- tag rm <item> <tag>
- claim <item>
- complete <item>
- priority <item> <0-255>
- delete <item>
- list
- show <item>
- events [limit]
- sync

Status semantics:

- status is monotonic open -> in_progress -> done.
- claim advances an item to in_progress.
- complete advances an item to done.
- There is no undo command. A mistaken completion is represented by creating a replacement item with a new row id.

Note editing:

- edit note <item> edits a single-line note through a line-editor style prompt prefilled with the current note. Multi-line notes are out of scope for this slice.

edit_count semantics:

- Increment edit_count implicitly whenever any CLI command changes a row value, including add, rename, edit note, tag add, tag rm, claim, complete, and priority.
- delete does not increment because the row disappears.

Events:

- No watch mode.
- events prints process-local event history with timestamps.
- events <limit> prints only the newest limit events.

Synchronisation:

- sync is explicit/manual. Local mutation commands update local durable state but do not intentionally push replication traffic until sync is invoked, so multi-machine scenarios can be stepped through deliberately.

title: T11 Define the replicated checklist row model
description:
Define the replicated checklist row model for the manual replication CLI example. Each checklist item is one replicated row keyed by a generated UUID. The CLI should display the full UUID and may use it directly in commands; this example does not need pretty ids.

Agreed schema proposal:

- text: LINEAR_STRING, default empty string. Main item label; concurrent text edits merge structurally.
- note: LINEAR_STRING, default empty string. Longer free-form notes; useful for concurrent text-edit scenarios.
- tags: LINEAR_LIST<STRING>, default empty list. Exercises list CRDT behaviour for concurrent tag insertions/deletions.
- status: TOTAL_ORDER_FINITE_STATE_REGISTER over open < in_progress < done, default open. Status is monotonic; un-done is represented by creating a copy/replacement item with a new row id.
- priority: LATEST_VALUE_WINS U8 NOT NULL, default 0. Freely editable priority where concurrent edits resolve by the register's LWW total order.
- edit_count: MONOTONIC_COUNTER, default 0. Increment implicitly whenever any CLI command changes a row value, including row creation. Row deletion does not need to increment because the row disappears.

This intentionally skips plain TOTAL_ORDER_REGISTER because status and edit_count already cover the more interesting ordered/monotonic behaviours without adding a redundant checklist concept.

Scenarios to flesh out with concrete CLI commands once the command surface exists:

- Concurrent text edits on the same item should exercise LINEAR_STRING merging.
- Concurrent note edits on the same item should exercise longer LINEAR_STRING merging.
- Concurrent tag insertions/deletions should exercise LINEAR_LIST<STRING> merging.
- Concurrent status transitions should converge to the furthest monotonic state.
- Concurrent priority edits should exercise LATEST_VALUE_WINS conflict resolution.
- Concurrent value-changing edits should accumulate edit_count increments.

title: T10 Add manual synchronisation control to the checklist example
description: Make the checklist example stage local edits without automatically synchronising every change immediately. Add an explicit manual command, such as sync, that triggers outbound replication so concurrent scenarios can be prepared on separate machines before the user presses Enter.

title: T09 Build the replicated checklist CLI example
description:
Add a very small replicated checklist CLI example that can run on two machines on a home network. It is configured entirely from one TOML config file passed as the only CLI argument. The config supplies local member identity, store path, fixed group id, ordered members, local endpoint bind address, and static peer routes. The app installs or loads exactly one configured static group, publishes checklist changes only on explicit sync, and shows replicated events clearly from a line-oriented terminal interface.

Agreed implementation plan:

G01. Add replicated_checklist as a real binary with exactly one positional argument: the config file path. No --member, no separate group id arg, no runtime flags.

G02. Define checklist app config inside that same TOML file, alongside the existing runtime keys. Required fields: local member identity, store path, fixed group id, ordered members. Runtime reachability stays under flotsync.replication.runtime.local-endpoint-bind-addr and [[flotsync.replication.runtime.static-peer-routes]].

G03. Parse and validate config before startup: group id must parse, local member must be present in ordered members, ordered members must be valid and duplicate-free, and static peer routes remain the existing UDP-only runtime config path.

G04. Add a second disk-backed SQLite constructor. Unsynced checklist edits stay process-local in the working copy as agreed; the store path is for runtime/group/update state that has actually been accepted durably.

G05. Install/load exactly one configured static group before loading the runtime. If the store already has the group, verify the configured group id, ordered members, and local member index match. If absent, insert the configured ReplicationGroupRecord with the configured group id and initial version vector. This can be removed later once mutable group support exists.

G06. Start the replication runtime from the configured store plus the same runtime config TOML. The app does not call create_group; it relies on the configured static group and startup store preparation.

G07. Make listener ingestion concurrency-safe with a listener-to-REPL queue, not by sharing ChecklistWorkingCopy across threads. The listener drains ReplicationEvent::DataChanged providers and sends row batches/events into a thread-safe channel. The REPL thread remains the only owner of ChecklistWorkingCopy.

G08. Keep inbound events buffered until sync. On sync: prepare dirty rows from the working copy, call publish_changes if dirty, clear dirty flags only after success, then drain the listener queue into the working copy and apply all queued events. This preserves the publish first, then drain incoming including our own publish result model.

G09. Wire the existing command surface only: item commands, list, show, events, sync, members, me, help, quit. members and me render config/runtime summary; they do not mutate group state.

G10. Keep tests light: config parsing/validation, static group preparation mismatch handling, and sync orchestration around the listener queue. The binary itself is the manual testing tool, so no heavy end-to-end harness.
close_reason: Replicated checklist binary is ready for manual testing

title: T03 Configure manual runtime peer endpoints
description:
Expose runtime-host configuration for manual peer endpoints through TOML/Kompact configuration, without adding a temporary public ReplicationApi route-management surface.

Agreed design:

S01. Replace the temporary runtime discovery source with PreconfiguredPeerRoutesComponent.

S02. PreconfiguredPeerRoutesComponent implements Provide<RouteDiscoveryPort<TransportRouteKey>) and publishes only configured peer routes. No automatic discovery behaviour.

S03. Configuration lives under flotsync.replication.runtime.static-peer-routes as TOML arrays of tables. Each peer route has name, protocol, ip, and port fields. The local bind remains flotsync.replication.runtime.local-endpoint-bind-addr.

S04. Supported protocol for this slice is udp only. Unknown protocols are invalid configuration.

S05. Add a reusable parser/pre-check around Kompact's parsed TOML config so bad static routes fail runtime startup before component execution.

S06. Extend the runtime-system builder path with an in-memory TOML string parameter that is merged into KompactConfig before the system is built. Applications can own file loading themselves.

S07. The route component listens to UdpPort indications and publishes configured peer routes after observing the local endpoint bind announcement from the bridge. This keeps route announcements sequenced after the runtime has a receive socket, without involving LocalEndpointManager explicitly.

S08. Static peer routes require a fixed local endpoint port in this slice. If static routes are configured with local-endpoint-bind-addr port 0, startup should fail.

S09. Tests may use constructor/test-only controls to make route publication timing deterministic, while production uses publish-on-local-endpoint-bound.

S10. Keep relay routes, TCP routes, route refresh loops, route health policy, and automatic discovery out of scope.
close_reason: Completed static peer route configuration for manual replication slice

title: Implement flotsync_security MVP for authenticated encrypted replication envelopes
description:
Baseline MVP for flotsync_security after planning discussion on 2026-05-14 and setup-boundary revision on 2026-05-16.

Goal: make replication delivery envelopes encrypted and signed by default, with external JWKS identity key files, group security state provisioned into ReplicationStore before runtime start, sensitive group-security columns encrypted with a device-local application database secret, helper key generation, replicated-checklist setup support, and scenario documentation for the secure first-run flow.

Non-goals for this MVP: discovery-advertised trust, certificates, key rotation, revocation, multi-shot HPKE sessions, relay-specific TCP session encryption, user passphrase handling, OS keyring storage for the database secret, and long-term migration UX. Those items are tracked as follow-up tasks rather than hidden in this umbrella.
design:
P01 Crate boundary: add a new workspace crate named flotsync_security. It owns key parsing, typed key material, key generation helpers, signing, verification, group encryption, HPKE bootstrap encryption, transcript construction, deterministic test-support identities, and crypto errors. It must not depend on Kompact, flotsync_io, delivery components, or store internals.

P02 Dependencies: flotsync_replication depends on flotsync_security. flotsync_security may depend on flotsync_core for member identifiers and on crypto/JWK crates. flotsync_messages and flotsync_io stay crypto-free. flotsync_discovery may later depend on flotsync_security for signed discovery, but not in this MVP.

P03 Setup boundary: flotsync_security does not parse application TOML or Kompact config. For this MVP slice, replicated-checklist setup reads a temporary plaintext local database secret, local private JWKS path, and trusted public JWKS paths from application config, validates them, and provisions ReplicationStore during ensure_configured_group before replication starts.

P04 Identity key files: use JWKS/JWK via jose-jwk. One local private JWKS contains two OKP keys: Ed25519 for signing and X25519 for encryption. A public JWKS contains the matching public keys and is easy to copy to peers. The implementation extracts the strict fields it supports and rejects unexpected or missing key roles.

P05 Key generation: flotsync_security exposes helper functions to generate a local identity JWKS and matching public JWKS for a MemberIdentity. The replicated-checklist CLI uses these helpers so users can bootstrap example setup without external crypto tooling.

P06 Trusted public identities: MVP trust is supplied to replicated-checklist setup as trusted member JWKS files. Setup validates configured static group members against those files and writes the accepted public keys into ReplicationStore.

P07 Sensitive group storage: add sensitive columns or an opaque sensitive BLOB next to existing replication_groups metadata. ReplicationGroupRecord should carry group security material because group membership, current group version state, and group key are normally needed together.

P08 Local database secret: sensitive group-security material is encrypted at rest with a device-local application database secret. For this MVP slice, replicated-checklist reads that secret from plaintext application config; OS-backed storage through keyring is a follow-up.

P09 Stored group-security material: the stored sensitive material carries the group symmetric key, group cipher suite, and group member public keys required to verify later group traffic without global config lookups.

P10 Group lifetime: the group id is the group version and key epoch. Membership or key changes happen by creating a new group id through migration. There is no separate group epoch field in this MVP.

P11 Group cipher: use ChaCha20-Poly1305 as the default symmetric AEAD for group messages. It has a 32-byte key, 12-byte nonce, and 16-byte authentication tag. Nonces are derived from existing envelope context instead of transmitted.

P12 Group nonce derivation: derive the nonce from a domain separator plus group id, frame kind, sender identity, and delivery message id. The nonce is public information by design and must be unique under a group key.

P13 Group AEAD authenticated data: include canonical public header bytes and frame kind as authenticated data. This binds the ciphertext to its routing context.

P14 Group signature transcript: Ed25519 signatures cover a domain separator, frame kind, canonical public header, and ciphertext including the AEAD tag. The signature proves the claimed sender signed this exact sealed envelope. The AEAD tag proves ciphertext and authenticated context were produced under the group key.

P15 Group per-message overhead: keep per-message security overhead to the 16-byte ChaCha20-Poly1305 tag plus the 64-byte Ed25519 signature, beyond existing protobuf framing. Do not repeat algorithm id, key id, group epoch, or nonce on every group message.

P16 Reliable bootstrap encryption: use HPKE single-shot encryption with X25519 for bootstrap and recipient-specific key transport. Each encrypted bootstrap envelope has one fresh ephemeral HPKE public key, one ciphertext with tag, and one Ed25519 sender signature.

P17 HPKE ephemeral keys: generate a fresh ephemeral key pair per recipient bootstrap envelope, use it once, transmit the ephemeral public key as HPKE encapsulated key material, and erase the ephemeral private key after sealing. Multi-shot HPKE contexts are explicitly deferred.

P18 Bootstrap payload: include group id, ordered members, each member Ed25519 public signing key, each member X25519 public encryption key, group cipher suite, and group symmetric key. Future migration metadata can extend this payload.

P19 Inbound bootstrap handling: decrypt with the local X25519 private key, verify the sender signature over the public header plus HPKE encapsulated key plus ciphertext, validate public keys against trusted keys provisioned during setup when available, write group security material through the encrypted sensitive-column path, and install group membership only after those checks pass.

P20 Group outbound path: replace placeholder encrypted payload handling with flotsync_security group sealing. The runtime loads group security material from ReplicationStore, encodes RuntimeMessage bytes, seals them with the stored group key and derived nonce, signs the resulting envelope, and submits the existing delivery wire frame.

P21 Group inbound path: delivery ingress can still use clear public headers for shallow routing. The semantic owner or runtime then verifies the signature, opens the AEAD payload with the stored group key, decodes RuntimeMessage, and only then applies or responds.

P22 Reliable ack signing: recipient acknowledgements must be signed by the recipient key. Sender-side reliable delivery only completes after verifying the ack signature against the expected recipient public key and message id.

P23 Security default: no insecure off mode in normal runtime. Tests use deterministic test identities and test group keys from flotsync_security test-support helpers rather than plaintext placeholder signatures.

P24 Example and docs: update replicated-checklist setup, CLI key generation commands, and scenario docs to show local database secret setup, local identity JWKS paths, trusted public JWKS paths, and store provisioning before replication starts.

P25 Implementation structure: the MVP is broken into child bd tasks for crate foundation, abandoned live config, store metadata, bootstrap, group traffic, reliable acks, example setup/docs, store-provisioning setup, OS keyring follow-up, and integration validation. Deferred security work is tracked as separate follow-up tasks.
acceptance_criteria: The MVP is considered ready when replication runtime messages are sealed and signed through flotsync_security, secure group bootstrap installs encrypted sensitive group-security material in ReplicationStore, recipient acknowledgements are signed and verified, replicated-checklist can generate JWKS identity files and provision security state from its setup config, updated scenarios document the secure first-run flow, and tests cover tampering, trust mismatch, bootstrap, group delivery, and ack spoofing.

title: T01 Define the manual replication slice contract
description:
Manual functional replication slice contract.

C01. The manual slice supports fixed-membership replication groups between live peers with direct UDP routes configured out of band.

C02. Group size is not fixed by the slice. Any group size supported by the current fixed-membership runtime should be valid when every member is manually configured and live.

C03. Peer reachability is configured through Kompact/runtime configuration, including local bind endpoint and manually supplied peer endpoints. Do not add a temporary public ReplicationApi route-management surface.

C04. Automatic discovery is out of scope. mDNS, peer tracking, reachability probing, relay routes, shared routes, and TCP routes remain later work.

C05. Security is out of scope for this slice. No real encryption or signing; post-slice authenticated/encrypted envelopes are tracked by flotsync-sec.

C06. Group creation covers only fixed initial membership. No change_group_membership, migration, invitations, schema epoch changes, or group close flow.

C07. Replication covers row-level checklist-style changes over the existing schema-operation mapping: insert/update/delete where delete semantics are safe once flotsync-a3v is handled.

C08. Local publish semantics remain: durable local acceptance plus outbound submission before PublishReceipt returns.

C09. Runtime robustness must be strong enough that expected delivery and ordering faults are handled explicitly rather than turning into terminal runtime state. The implementation work for removing the runtime component's set_terminal-style mechanics is owned by flotsync-4x8 and blocks moving beyond this contract.

C10. Delivery robustness required for this slice includes ReliableDelivery sender-side recipient-ack timeout handling and non-terminal handling of expected transient inbound ordering races.

C11. The example app must allow manual synchronisation, so local edits can be staged on multiple peers and flushed with an explicit sync command.

C12. Offline catch-up, Summary/NeedRange/Snapshot/Ack flows, replay after extended disconnection, and relay/mailbox repair are out of scope.

C13. Functional acceptance means manually configured machines can create or join an arbitrary-size fixed group, make checklist edits, manually sync, observe remote events, and preserve durable local state across restart.

C14. Manual test scenarios include concurrent adds, done-vs-rename, delete-vs-edit, restart persistence, and visible static peer configuration.
close_reason: Contract agreed and recorded in the bead

title: Complete the manual functional replication slice
description:
Aligned scope for the manual functional replication slice, explicitly before automatic discovery.

Security follow-up moved out of this parent: authenticated and encrypted replication envelopes are tracked separately in flotsync-sec and are blocked by completion of this slice.

Current scope decision: live UpdateBatch broadcasts in this slice are best-effort. Durable GroupBroadcast work only belongs with the future relay or mailbox path, so broadcast delivery-state persistence is deferred out of this slice.

S01. Manual-slice contract: fixed-membership replication groups of arbitrary size, live peers, direct UDP routes configured out-of-band, no automatic discovery, no migration, and no relay or offline catch-up.

S02. Manual reachability via configuration is complete through TOML/Kompact runtime configuration and static UDP peer routes.

S03. Reliable bootstrap robustness is complete via flotsync-46r. ReliableDelivery now has sender-side recipient-ack timeout retry for bootstrap work that would otherwise remain stuck awaiting semantic acknowledgement.

S04. Runtime delivery robustness is complete via flotsync-4x8. Expected protocol-boundary noise, including live UpdateBatch delivery before local group installation, is logged and dropped instead of poisoning the runtime.

S05. Update mapping for the current row/schema operation path is complete via flotsync-94d. This covers UpdateId derivation, read_versions rules, group binding, compatibility checks, and inbound validation needed by the manual slice.

S06. Remaining correctness work: flotsync-a3v must separate replication tombstones from row garbage collection because delete-vs-edit is part of the checklist scenarios.

S07. Runnable checklist example, manual synchronisation control, checklist row model, and CLI command surface are complete through the flotsync-5j0 child tasks for the example.

S08. Remaining acceptance work: flotsync-5j0.9 must exercise the manual checklist scenarios after delete semantics are safe enough for the delete-vs-edit case.

S09. Deferred protocol and delivery work: durable GroupBroadcast relay state, common outer envelopes, full Summary/NeedRange/Snapshot/Ack catch-up, migration, rich group lifecycle modelling, and security hardening are future work outside this slice.

title: Replace InMemoryData schema Cow with SchemaSource
description: flotsync_data_types::schema::datamodel::InMemoryData currently stores its schema as Cow<'static, Schema>, while SchemaSource lives in flotsync_replication::api. This split is causing unnecessary schema cloning at call sites that need to cross between the two representations, for example where SchemaSource gets turned back into an owned Schema before building InMemoryData. Create a dedicated change to move SchemaSource into the datamodel area and switch InMemoryData to store SchemaSource directly instead of Cow<'static, Schema>. The work should cover constructors and snapshot/decode helpers, update affected call sites, and preserve the existing static-versus-shared ownership behaviour while avoiding avoidable schema clones.
close_reason: Completed; SchemaSource moved into datamodel and InMemoryData now stores SchemaSource directly while preserving static/shared schema ownership.

title: Separate replication tombstones from row garbage collection
description:
Current manual-slice blocker for delete semantics.

Current row-level delete handling physically removes rows from storage. Replication correctness needs durable tombstones to preserve delete causality independently from later row garbage collection.

Required for flotsync-5j0: the checklist delete-vs-edit scenario must not lose the delete intent or allow a concurrent edit to resurrect a deleted row incorrectly. Split durable replication tombstone semantics from eventual row cleanup in the replication store and runtime apply path. Later garbage collection remains a separate follow-up.

Concrete implementation plan:

P01. Model durable row state explicitly. Replace the current Option<RowSnapshot> storage view with a tombstone-aware row state so None means absent and Some(tombstoned=true) means deleted but causally retained.

P02. Stop treating replication deletes as physical storage deletes. Runtime delete application should persist the post-delete row snapshot plus a tombstone flag. Physical removal is future garbage collection work.

P03. Extend SQLite dataset_rows with a tombstone flag that defaults to false for existing rows, and make load/apply round-trip that flag.

P04. Add a small flotsync_data_types API for rebuilding InMemoryData rows as tombstoned from snapshots, plus a helper to query whether an existing row is tombstoned.

P05. Change local delete handling to persist a tombstoned row while still emitting RowChange::Delete.

P06. Change inbound apply visibility semantics: active update emits Upsert, active delete emits Delete, tombstoned-row update persists silently, and repeated tombstoned delete is idempotent and silent.

P07. Allow applied inbound operations to carry no listener-visible RowChange while still producing durable row patches.

P08. Add focused tests for SQLite tombstone round-trip, runtime restart preserving tombstones, inbound update-after-delete not resurrecting a row, and repeated/concurrent delete idempotence.

P09. Keep the checklist app unchanged unless the focused tests show a small adapter fix is required.
notes:
2026-04-30 implementation update: keep tombstone metadata out of bare RowSnapshot, introduce a documented RowRecord for retained row images, replace StoredDatasetRow with replication aliases around RowRecord, rename DatasetRowWrite cases to UpsertActive/UpsertTombstone, allow missing -> tombstone while rejecting tombstone -> active in the store layer, remove the unused physical delete/GC design, remove the SQLite migration helper because no durable databases exist yet, and add focused tests for tombstone storage semantics.
2026-04-30 implementation result: introduced RowRecord and replication row aliases, replaced DatasetRowWrite::Put/Delete with UpsertActive/UpsertTombstone, added SQLite transition validation that allows missing -> tombstone and rejects tombstone -> active, removed the SQLite tombstone migration helper, and added focused store/runtime/datamodel coverage. Created follow-up flotsync-uvo for the public ReplicationStore conformance suite. Local validation passed: cargo fmt --all; cargo test -p flotsync_data_types -- --test-threads=1; cargo test -p flotsync_replication -- --test-threads=1; cargo check --workspace --all-targets; git diff --check.

title: Reduce replication runtime dataset loading to touched rows
description: The runtime currently materialises whole datasets into memory during transactional publish/apply flows. This was not the intended design and causes both unnecessary memory use and extra copying. Follow up on flotsync-73q.4 by changing the storage/runtime boundary so transactional work can load and write back only the rows actually needed for the current operation, instead of full dataset images.
notes:
Implementation plan for flotsync-i5b:

1. Replace the snapshot-shaped runtime store API with row-granular transaction methods:
- DatasetRowSlice { group_id, dataset_id, dataset_exists, rows: Vec<(RowKey, Option<RowSnapshot>)> }
- DatasetRowPatch { group_id, dataset_id, actions: Vec<DatasetRowWrite> }
- DatasetRowWrite::{Put, Delete}
- load_dataset_rows(group_id, dataset_id, row_keys)
- apply_dataset_row_patch(patch)

2. Remove load_dataset_snapshot/apply_dataset_snapshot from the hot runtime path for now.

3. Keep schemas keyed by DatasetId for this task. dataset_exists stays explicit on the read side so the runtime can distinguish an empty existing dataset from a first-touch dataset.

4. Refactor the runtime helper layer so publish/apply only hold highly ephemeral in-memory slices for the touched rows. No resident full dataset cache and no full before/after diffing.

5. Local publish and inbound apply both load only touched row keys, mutate those partial in-memory slices, persist explicit Put/Delete row actions, and commit together with update-log/VV changes.

6. Add store/runtime tests for partial row load-write, untouched-row preservation, and rollback of row-granular writes.

title: Move replication runtime tests onto the in-memory sqlite store
description: Replace the current StoreStub-based runtime tests with the in-memory rusqlite ReplicationStore backend. Add fixtures for static-schema and Arc-schema sources, and cover restart recovery, rollback on failed transactions, durable PublishReceipt semantics, persisted dataset_updates, and causal buffering across reloads.
acceptance_criteria: The replication runtime test suite exercises the new store-backed path end-to-end, including restart and rollback cases, and no longer depends on the old non-persistent store stub for primary coverage.

title: Persist dataset_updates and apply causally ready chains transactionally
description: Replace the in-memory pending update queue with persisted dataset_updates records for inbound and outbound replication traffic. Support efficient lookup of not-yet-applied candidates, load those candidates into memory when checking causal readiness, and commit an entire newly-ready apply chain atomically so failures do not leave partially advanced replication state behind.
acceptance_criteria: Inbound and outbound updates are durably recorded, duplicate and conflict detection use persisted records, and applying one update together with any newly-ready successors is all-or-nothing at the transaction boundary.

title: Hydrate replication_groups and membership snapshots from ReplicationStore
description: Refactor runtime startup so persisted replication group metadata and canonical member snapshots are loaded from ReplicationStore, then used to seed SharedGroupMemberships. Remove LocalRuntimeState as the whole-runtime source of truth and keep only derived read models plus transaction-scoped metadata in the component.
acceptance_criteria: Runtime startup can recover persisted replication groups after restart, SharedGroupMemberships is derived from store state, and the component no longer treats its own in-memory map as the authoritative group registry.

title: Implement an in-memory sqlx ReplicationStore backend for the replication slice
description: Add a sqlx-backed ReplicationStore implementation that runs entirely in named shared-cache SQLite memory mode so tests stay isolated and concurrent-safe. Share an Arc<SqlitePool> across the store, acquire a dedicated connection per transaction, configure the pool so the in-memory database stays alive, and persist the replication slice using immutable group membership, a version-vector blob, row-wise dataset persistence driven by the aligned storage action API, and immutable UpdateBatch blobs with separately tracked applied state.
acceptance_criteria: The backend opens without creating on-disk files, initialises its schema automatically, keeps the named in-memory database alive through the pool, begins write transactions with BEGIN IMMEDIATE, preserves full u64 update ordering, enforces the agreed group and update immutability rules, reuses shared codec helpers instead of duplicating encode/decode logic, and is usable as the primary test store for runtime integration tests.
notes:
Backend plan aligned with review:
107. Use sqlx SQLite in named shared in-memory mode, with an Arc<SqlitePool> shared by the store and a dedicated acquired connection per transaction.
108. Configure the pool so the in-memory database stays alive, then begin each write transaction with BEGIN IMMEDIATE.
109. Keep substantial SQL as static strings and rely on sqlx's per-connection statement cache; if hot statements need warming, do that in after_connect rather than pretending a global sqlx::query value carries prepared-statement state.
110. Store group membership as immutable rows plus a stored member_count, and allow only the version_vector blob to change on an existing group.
111. Store each UpdateBatch as one immutable blob; only the applied flag may change after insertion.
112. Preserve full u64 update ordering by storing versions in fixed-width big-endian blobs, with explicit comments explaining why SQLite's lexicographic BLOB ordering is correct here.
113. Replace whole-dataset snapshot blobs with row-wise persistence driven by the explicit storage action API.

title: Define transactional ReplicationStore boundary for the first replication slice
description: Expand ReplicationStore from identity/schema lookup into an object-safe, async-safe transaction boundary for the first replication slice. Cover replication group metadata, canonical members and member indices, version vectors, dataset snapshot access, persisted dataset update records, and schema lookup that can return either shared Arc-backed schemas or static schemas. Keep the scope deliberately to the current replication runtime needs rather than the full long-term storage abstraction.
acceptance_criteria: A concrete trait and DTO shape exists for first-slice replication state, transaction semantics are explicit about read-your-own-writes plus commit/rollback, and the schema source can represent Arc or &'static schemas without forcing extra cloning.

title: Remove terminal runtime fault handling for expected inbound delivery errors
description:
The manual replication slice must not record terminal runtime state for expected delivery and ordering faults. The current runtime component uses set_terminal-style mechanics and fails subsequent API calls after inbound group-delivery errors. That was acceptable as a conservative first-slice guard, but it is too strong for the manual functional slice.

Agreed handling policy:

R01. Remove the runtime component's terminal_fault / set_terminal-style mechanics entirely, including RuntimeTerminated API failure behaviour.

R02. API calls should fail only when the component is actually unavailable, using the normal ask failure path.

R03. Production protocol-boundary noise should log and drop: unknown group updates, wrong runtime message kind on a delivery channel, and corrupt runtime payloads.

R04. Test-only protocol-boundary noise may panic or otherwise fail loudly instead of growing elaborate result plumbing.

R05. Unknown group updates are treated as not interested in this group. Drop with logging; do not buffer pre-bootstrap races.

R06. Reliable-delivery messages that cannot be decoded or accepted must not complete the processed promise. Ack/retry behaviour remains with flotsync-46r.

R07. Store access failures from inbound delivery are fatal for now. A closed or corrupted database should not leave a ghost component that only logs repeated failures.

R08. Listener errors are treated as permanent boundary failures. The runtime does not own listener retry and may terminate/fault on listener failure.

R09. Conflicting duplicate payloads should log loudly, not apply, not advance state, and not kill the runtime.

R10. No custom Kompact recovery is required in this task. Stop self-poisoning expected errors; let real panics/component faults flow through normal Kompact supervision.

R11. Keep tests minimal: prove an unknown-group inbound update does not poison later API calls, and keep existing direct error tests where useful. Avoid an exhaustive corrupt-payload matrix.

title: Add sender-side recipient-ack timeout retry to ReliableDelivery
description:
ReliableDelivery currently leaves sender work items stuck in AwaitingRecipientAck when a recipient never completes semantic processing or rejects an inbound message before acking. Add a sender-side timeout that re-enters the existing retry path when no recipient ack is observed, and update runtime/bootstrap handling to surface failures with context. Discovered while reviewing the first replication slice runtime on flotsync-75s.

Agreed implementation plan:

P01. Add a recipient-ack timeout config key flotsync.reliable-delivery.recipient-ack-timeout with a five-minute production default. Tests may override it to a short duration.

P02. After an outbound reliable envelope transport send succeeds, move the sender work item to AwaitingRecipientAck and schedule its sender retry key for recipient-ack-timeout.

P03. When that retry key fires while the work item is still AwaitingRecipientAck and RecipientAckStatus is still Pending, log the timeout, move the item back to PendingRoute, and reuse the normal direct dispatch path. The retry sends the same message_id and payload, not a new logical reliable message.

P04. Recipient acks may arrive late while the sender work item is PendingRoute or AttemptingDirect after a timeout-driven retry. Accept them for any existing sender work item, cancel the sender retry key, and mark RecipientAckStatus::Observed.

P05. Make receiver duplicate handling state-aware. If a duplicate inbound envelope arrives while AwaitingProcessed, drop it because the receiver already has the envelope locally. If it arrives while AckPending, retry/schedule the cached recipient ack. If it arrives while AckInFlight, drop it because an ack send is already in progress. If the receiver already sent the ack and removed inbound state, a later resend is processed as fresh; bootstrap installation is idempotent for the same group definition and will ack again.

P06. Update the runtime bootstrap processed-promise comment to document the contract: processed is completed only after durable bootstrap install; failures intentionally withhold processed so the sender-side recipient-ack timeout drives redelivery.

P07. Add tests for timeout redelivery, late ack acceptance after a timeout retry, and receiver duplicate handling. Verify with focused ReliableDelivery tests, the full flotsync_replication test suite, and clippy.

title: Investigate flaky Linux workspace test failures in pr-linux CI
description: PR #2 hit a flaky failure in the pr-linux GitHub Actions workflow on April 12, 2026 (run 24302768196, job 70958911092). The failing tests were driver::udp::tests::udp_connected_send_and_receive_work, driver::udp::tests::udp_unconnected_bind_send_and_receive_work, kompact::tests::tcp_listener_reuse_config_allows_binding_to_a_reserved_port, and kompact::tests::udp_bridge_broadcasts_socket_activity_but_send_results_stay_private. The failure did not reproduce locally with cargo test -p flotsync_io or cargo test --workspace on macOS, so this likely needs investigation on Linux or in a Linux-like environment. Capture a deterministic reproduction path if possible, then stabilise or redesign the flaky socket/runtime test expectations.

title: Standardise async test support with eventually-style waits and shared harnesses
description: Create one shared test-support layer for flotsync_io, flotsync_udpour, and flotsync_replication that replaces the current ad-hoc polling loops and fragmented harness code. Scope includes an eventually-style helper for predicate-based waiting, shared buffered probe utilities for mpsc-backed event streams, common Kompact lifecycle helpers, and reusable transport/harness fixtures so crates stop re-implementing near-identical test infrastructure.
acceptance_criteria: There is one sanctioned eventually-style wait helper used across the targeted crates; duplicated buffered receiver and polling helpers are removed or folded into shared support; new or migrated tests use the shared harness abstractions rather than crate-local bespoke fixtures.

title: Serialise socket-heavy test harnesses across crates
description: The current replication and IO integration tests use real local UDP sockets and rely on ad-hoc serialisation. Evaluate project-level test serialisation and/or a shared cross-process lock so socket-heavy tests do not interfere across crates or test binaries. This was discovered while implementing flotsync-w6r.

title: Fix egress adopt_payload to accept payloads already owned by the same pool
description: EgressPool::adopt_payload currently rejects pooled IoPayload fragments once they have been cloned, even when those fragments are already owned by the same egress pool. UDPour currently has to fall back to copying in this case. Adjust the adopt logic so payloads already retargeted to the destination pool are accepted instead of treated as shared ownership failures.

title: Add mockable clocks for UDPour timing tests
description: Replace real sleeps in UDPour timing tests with mockable or controllable clocks/timers so tombstone expiry, pacing, and retry behavior can be tested deterministically without wall-clock waits.
design:
Goal
- Make Kompact runtimes testable against a manually advanced timer without changing the default production behaviour.
- Preserve current scheduling semantics for one-shot, periodic, cancellation, and zero-delay timers.

Chosen Route
- Keep the existing Kompact config seam at `KompactConfig::timer(...)`.
- Preserve Kompact's concrete `timer::TimerRef` path by teaching `hierarchical_hash_wheel_timer` to expose one queue-based timer reference that can be driven either by a dedicated thread or by a manual driver.
- Add a test-oriented manual timer component in Kompact that wraps the manual driver and exposes a separate control handle to the harness.

P1. Timer Crate Refactor
- Extract the queue message type, timer-reference construction, and schedule/cancel plumbing out of `thread_timer.rs` into a shared queue-driven core.
- Keep `thread_timer::TimerRef` as the public scheduling handle so downstream API churn stays minimal.
- Refactor `TimerWithThread` to delegate to that shared core plus a thread loop.
- Add a new manual driver type, tentatively `ManualTimer` or `DrivenTimer`, with `timer_ref()`, `advance_by`, `advance_one`, `advance_until_idle`, `current_time`, and one lightweight observability method such as `is_idle` or `next_due_in`.
- Ensure queued schedule/cancel messages are drained both before and during advancement so commands issued between advances, or triggered indirectly during execution, are observed deterministically.
- Define zero-delay semantics explicitly and match the threaded behaviour as closely as possible.

P2. Timer Crate Validation
- Add parity tests that run equivalent scripted schedule/cancel sequences against both the threaded and manual drivers.
- Cover one-shot timers, periodic timers, cancellation before fire, cancellation after enqueue, zero-delay timers, large skips, multiple timers expiring on the same tick, and schedule-from-trigger behaviour.
- Add explicit tests for `advance_until_idle` so pathological periodic workloads fail loudly instead of spinning silently.
- Document any deliberate semantic difference that cannot be eliminated.

P3. Kompact Integration
- Add a `ManualTimerComponent` that stores shared manual-timer state and implements `TimerComponent` by returning the shared queue-based `TimerRef`.
- Add a companion `ManualTimerControl` handle for tests. Harnesses should keep this handle outside the runtime and use it to advance time.
- Keep the production default unchanged; tests opt in by installing the manual timer through `KompactConfig::timer(...)`.
- If useful upstream, add a small helper constructor that returns the builder closure together with the retained control handle.
- Avoid widening Kompact abstractions unless the shared-reference route proves insufficient.
- Fallback if necessary: generalise `TimerRefFactory` and `TimerManager` away from the concrete `timer::TimerRef` alias as a second-stage patch.

P4. Kompact Validation
- Add system-level tests showing that scheduled timeouts do not fire until manual time advances past the deadline.
- Cover periodic rescheduling, cancellation, dropping components with pending timers, and runtime shutdown with queued timeouts.
- Add a multi-component test to verify deterministic delivery when several components share the same manual timer.
- Document the contract between time advancement and component execution, because advancing time will enqueue work but may still require the harness to wait for component turns to run.

P5. Flotsync Adoption
- Extend the local Kompact test builders so they can install the manual timer and return its control handle.
- Migrate the timing-sensitive UDPour runtime tests in this issue first: pacing, retries, retention or tombstone expiry, and similar wall-clock-driven cases.
- Use the shared eventually-style helper from `flotsync-6uy` for post-advance observation. The manual timer removes real sleeps; it does not remove the need to wait for asynchronous side effects to become visible.
- Keep a smaller set of real-time smoke tests where the purpose is explicitly to cover threaded scheduling and integration with the live runtime.

P6. Risks And Upstreaming Notes
- The existing `simulation::SimulationTimer` is not a direct fit for Kompact because it is not queue-reference based and relies on `Rc`, so it should inform semantics rather than be wrapped directly.
- The most delicate semantic area is commands racing with `advance_by`; those rules need to be explicit and tested.
- Prefer an upstreamable manual-driver abstraction in the timer crate over a flotsync-only test shim.

Done Means
- A Kompact runtime can be built with a manual timer and externally advanced deterministically.
- The UDPour timing tests covered by `flotsync-jgg` stop depending on wall-clock sleeps for timer progression.
- The default threaded timer remains the production default and retains its current behaviour.

title: Prevent duplicate UDPour delivery from late retransmissions
